items.q: What's the difference between monthly, quarterly, and semi-annual?
items.a: Monthly is a recurring subscription — cancel anytime. Quarterly is a one-time payment, valid for 3 months with no auto-renewal. Semi-annual is a one-time payment, valid for 6 months with no auto-renewal. All new users get a 7-day Pro trial on signup, no credit card required.

items.q: Do I need a credit card?
items.a: No credit card is needed to sign up — the 7-day trial is completely free. When you're ready to subscribe, payments are securely processed via Stripe.

Cancel anytime via the Stripe customer portal (click "Manage Subscription" on the pricing page). Paid users keep Pro access until the end of the billing cycle. Quarterly and semi-annual plans do not auto-renew — no need to cancel manually. Full refund within 24 hours of first payment (one-time only).
We accept Visa, Mastercard, American Express, and other major credit/debit cards, as well as Apple Pay, Klarna, and Affirm — all securely processed through Stripe (PCI DSS Level 1 certified).

items.q: Can you guarantee I'll reach CLB 7+?
items.a: We do not guarantee any exam results. HiTCF provides high-quality practice aligned with TCF Canada to help you identify and improve weak areas. Final scores depend on many factors. See our disclaimer for details.

items.q: Are these the same as the real exam questions?
items.a: Practice questions are compiled and edited from publicly available materials, covering levels A1–C2 and formatted to match TCF Canada. However, this platform is not officially affiliated, and questions are not identical to actual exam items.

items.q: How do I request a refund?
items.a: You can request a full refund within 24 hours of your first payment (one-time only). Simply email support@hitcf.com. See our refund policy for details.

exam.desc: TCF Canada (Test de connaissance du français pour le Canada) is a French proficiency test developed by France Éducation international (formerly CIEP), designed specifically for Canadian immigration and citizenship applications.
speakingTasks: Role play (5.5 minutes, incl. 2 min prep)

scores.title: NCLC / CLB Score Conversion Table
scores.subtitle: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) uses NCLC / CLB levels to assess language proficiency. Below is the TCF Canada score conversion.
scores.footnote: * NCLC 7 is the minimum language requirement for most Express Entry and PNP programs, and the target level for most test-takers
scores.nclc7Desc: NCLC 7 (i.e. CLB 7) is the minimum French requirement for most Canadian economic immigration programs. Reaching NCLC 7 means you meet the language threshold for Express Entry (CEC, FSW) and most Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP). Higher NCLC levels (8, 9, 10) earn additional CRS points — NCLC 9 gives the maximum language bonus.

learn.title: Free French Study Resources
learn.subtitle: All free or official resources to help you improve your French from every angle

official.desc: Free French learning platform by the French international TV network. Video courses + interactive exercises for all levels from A1 to C1, organized by themes like culture, society, and science.
French international radio learning channel. Real French news audio with practice exercises. Various speech speeds from slow to normal — excellent TCF listening prep material.
RFI's 'Easy French News' — 10-minute clear, slow-paced daily international news broadcasts. Listen daily to steadily improve your listening comprehension.
Official TCF test organization website. View exam descriptions, scoring criteria, and official sample questions. Essential to understand the real exam format before preparing.
Long-established French learning site with extensive free grammar, vocabulary, listening and reading exercises graded by difficulty. Great for building solid grammar foundations.

Hugo's French learning platform with podcasts, YouTube videos, and courses. Natural French discussing cultural and social topics. Moderate pace with depth — ideal for intermediate learners to break through plateaus.

youtube.desc: Million-subscriber immersive French channel. Johan speaks only French about everyday topics — no grammar rules, just natural input to develop intuition.
French native Dylane's teaching channel focused on grammar explanations and vocabulary expansion. Short, clear, and well-organized episodes.
French teacher Pierre's comprehensive channel covering grammar, vocabulary, pronunciation, and culture. French audio with subtitles — perfect for intensive listening.
Hugo's pure French podcast channel. 20–30 minute episodes discussing cultural and social topics. Moderate pace, great for extensive listening and building speaking/writing material.
Street interview series in France. Real everyday French from native speakers with French-English subtitles. Authentic expressions and spoken rhythm you won't find in textbooks.
Bilingual English-French teaching channel. Alexa explains French grammar and pronunciation in English. Slow pace with lots of examples — perfect for absolute beginners up to A2.

tools.name: Bilibili
tools.desc: Search for 'French A1 textbook audio' or 'TCF prep' — tons of French learning videos with Chinese explanations. The most accessible resource for Chinese-speaking learners.

authority.body: HiTCF is designed to match the official TCF Canada exam format (CO / CE / EE / EO) administered by <fei>France Éducation International (FEI)</fei>. TCF Canada scores are recognized by <ircc>Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC)</ircc> as proof of French proficiency for permanent residence and citizenship applications. HiTCF is an independent third-party preparation platform not affiliated with FEI or IRCC.

credibility.body: The platform hosts 1,306 test sets and 8,397 questions: 42 listening sets (1,638 questions), 42 reading sets (1,638 questions), 702 speaking topic sets (3,536 questions), and 520 writing task sets (1,540 questions), covering CEFR A1 through C2. Every listening passage has sentence-level timestamps generated by OpenAI Whisper, speaking is evaluated by Azure Speech + Grok across 6 TCF dimensions, and writing is graded on the official 4-criteria TCF rubric. New users get a 7-day Pro trial — no credit card required.

tcfCanada.heading: What Is TCF Canada? A Complete Guide
tcfCanada.intro: The TCF Canada (Test de connaissance du français pour le Canada) is the official French language proficiency test recognized by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). Whether you are applying for permanent residence or Canadian citizenship, understanding this exam is your first step toward success.

whatIs.title: What Is the TCF Canada Exam?
whatIs.p1: The TCF Canada is administered by France Education international (formerly CIEP) and is specifically designed for immigration purposes to Canada. Unlike the general TCF, the TCF Canada evaluates all four language skills: listening, reading, speaking, and writing. It provides results mapped to the Niveaux de compétence linguistique canadiens (NCLC), which are equivalent to the Canadian Language Benchmarks (CLB) used by IRCC.
whatIs.p2: The test is available at authorized test centers worldwide and can be taken multiple times. Results are valid for two years from the test date. Many candidates take the test more than once to improve their scores, making regular practice essential.

structure.title: Exam Structure: 4 Sections
structure.intro: The TCF Canada consists of four mandatory sections. The listening and reading sections are computer-based multiple choice, while speaking and writing are evaluated by certified examiners.

co.title: Listening Comprehension (Compréhension orale — CO)
co.desc: 39 multiple-choice questions to be completed in approximately 35 minutes. Questions progress from simple everyday dialogues (A1) to complex academic lectures and debates (C2). Each audio recording is played only once during the exam, so active listening skills are critical.

ce.title: Reading Comprehension (Compréhension écrite — CE)
ce.desc: 39 multiple-choice questions to be completed in approximately 60 minutes. Texts range from short signs and notices (A1) to lengthy academic or literary passages (C2). You will encounter advertisements, emails, newspaper articles, essays, and research abstracts.

eo.title: Speaking (Expression orale — EO)
eo.desc: 3 tasks completed in approximately 12 minutes, recorded and evaluated by examiners. Task 1 is a guided interview, Task 2 is a role-play interaction, and Task 3 requires expressing and defending an opinion on a complex topic.

ee.title: Writing (Expression écrite — EE)
ee.desc: 3 writing tasks to be completed in approximately 60 minutes. Task 1 is a short message or email (60-120 words), Task 2 is an article or letter on a given topic (120-150 words), and Task 3 is a comparative essay analyzing two perspectives (120-180 words).

scoring.title: Scoring System
scoring.p1: Each section is scored independently on a scale corresponding to NCLC levels 1 through 12+. For the listening and reading sections, a numerical score from 0 to 699 is assigned, which maps to a specific NCLC level. Speaking and writing are scored directly on the NCLC scale by trained evaluators.
scoring.p2: Your TCF Canada attestation will show four separate scores — one for each section. IRCC uses these individual scores (not an average) to assess your French proficiency. Different immigration programs have different minimum requirements per section.

nclc.title: NCLC/CLB Equivalence
nclc.desc: NCLC (Niveaux de compétence linguistique canadiens) is the French equivalent of CLB (Canadian Language Benchmarks). NCLC 7 equals CLB 7, which is the most commonly required level for Express Entry programs. For listening and reading, NCLC 7 corresponds to a score of approximately 458-502 out of 699. For speaking and writing, you need to be assessed at NCLC 7 or higher by the examiners.

immigration.title: Why TCF Canada Matters for Immigration
immigration.p1: French language proficiency is a significant advantage in the Canadian immigration process. Under the Express Entry Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS), strong French scores can earn you substantial additional points — up to 50 bonus points for CLB 7+ in French as a second official language, plus base language points.
immigration.p2: For the Programme régulier des travailleurs qualifiés (PRTQ) in Quebec, the TCF Canada is one of the accepted tests for demonstrating French proficiency. Many Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) also accept TCF Canada results. Achieving CLB 7+ in all four skills is the key benchmark that most applicants target.

cta.title: Start Practicing Today
cta.desc: HiTCF offers 8,500+ TCF Canada practice questions with sentence-by-sentence audio replay, AI-powered speaking and writing feedback, and detailed analytics to track your progress toward CLB 7+.

clb7.heading: How to Reach CLB 7+ on TCF Canada
clb7.intro: CLB 7 (NCLC 7) is the most important benchmark for Canadian immigration. Reaching this level across all four skills can significantly boost your CRS score and open doors to permanent residence. This guide breaks down exactly what you need and how to get there.

whatIsClb7.title: What Does CLB 7 Mean?
whatIsClb7.p1: CLB 7 (Canadian Language Benchmark 7) represents an intermediate-to-advanced level of language proficiency. In the context of TCF Canada, it corresponds to NCLC 7. For the listening and reading sections, this typically requires a score of around 458-502 out of 699, which translates to roughly 78% accuracy across all difficulty levels.
whatIsClb7.p2: CLB 7+ in all four skills is the threshold used by IRCC for maximum Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) points under Express Entry. Even if you are a primarily English-speaking applicant, demonstrating CLB 7+ in French as a second official language can earn you up to 50 additional CRS points — often the difference between receiving an Invitation to Apply (ITA) and waiting another round.

targetScores.title: Target Scores Per Section
targetScores.intro: Here are the approximate scores you need to reach NCLC 7 in each section:
targetScores.co: Listening (CO): 458-502 out of 699 — You need to correctly answer roughly 30 out of 39 questions, including most B1-B2 level questions.
targetScores.ce: Reading (CE): 458-502 out of 699 — Similar to listening, aim for about 30 correct answers with solid performance on mid-level texts.
targetScores.eo: Speaking (EO): NCLC 7 — Examiners assess fluency, vocabulary range, grammatical accuracy, pronunciation, and task completion across all 3 tasks.
targetScores.ee: Writing (EE): NCLC 7 — Your writing must demonstrate clear organization, varied vocabulary, generally accurate grammar, and appropriate register for each task type.

listening.title: Listening Strategies
listening.desc: Build a daily listening habit with authentic French audio: podcasts (RFI Journal en français facile, France Info), news broadcasts, and TCF practice recordings. Focus on understanding the main idea and key details on first listen, since the exam plays each recording only once. Use HiTCF's sentence-by-sentence replay feature to analyze passages you found difficult. Train your ear to distinguish French phonemes, liaisons, and enchaînements. Practice under timed conditions regularly to build stamina.

reading.title: Reading Strategies
reading.desc: Read French daily — newspapers (Le Monde, Le Devoir), magazines, and online articles covering various topics. For the exam, learn to identify key information quickly: scan for topic sentences, numbers, proper nouns, and logical connectors. Practice time management by allocating roughly 1.5 minutes per question. For higher-level texts (B2-C1), focus on understanding implicit meanings, author's tone, and argumentative structures. Build vocabulary systematically using flashcards and spaced repetition.

speaking.title: Speaking Strategies
speaking.desc: Practice speaking French every day, even if just 10 minutes of self-talk. For Task 1 (interview), prepare answers about your background, work, studies, and daily life. For Task 2 (role play), practice common scenarios: making complaints, negotiating, requesting information. For Task 3 (opinion), develop a framework: state your position, give 2-3 arguments with examples, acknowledge counterarguments, and conclude. Use HiTCF's AI conversation feature to simulate exam-like interactions and get instant feedback on pronunciation and fluency.

writing.title: Writing Strategies
writing.desc: Write something in French every day — even short journal entries count. For Task 1, master email conventions (formal vs. informal register, salutations, closing formulas). For Task 2, practice structuring articles with clear introductions, developed paragraphs, and conclusions. For Task 3, learn the comparative essay format: present each viewpoint fairly before giving your own opinion. Focus on grammatical accuracy (verb conjugations, agreements, prepositions) and vary your vocabulary. Always proofread your work — allocate 5 minutes at the end for corrections.

howHitcfHelps.intro: HiTCF is designed specifically to help TCF Canada candidates achieve their target scores efficiently:
howHitcfHelps.feature1: 8,500+ practice questions covering all four skills, organized by difficulty level (A1-C2) so you can target your weak areas.

tcfListening.heading: TCF Canada Listening (CO) Complete Guide: A1 to C2 Section-by-Section Strategy
tcfListening.intro: TCF Canada Listening (Compréhension orale, or CO) is the most underestimated and easily-lost section of the exam. 39 questions in 35 minutes, audio played only once, spanning A1 café chats to C2 academic lectures without a pause. This guide is built on data from HiTCF's 42 full listening test sets (1,638 real questions) and gives you a stage-by-stage strategy, scoring weight table, trap checklist, and an 8-week study plan you can follow straight away.

quickFacts.title: Exam at a glance (memorize these 5 numbers)
quickFacts.audio: Each clip plays exactly once — no replay
quickFacts.difficultyRange: A1 to C2, strictly ascending order

format.title: Exam Format: One pass, no going back
format.p1: The TCF Canada listening section has 39 multiple-choice questions, each with 4 options (A/B/C/D). Total duration is about 35 minutes — roughly 3 minutes of official instructions and 32 minutes of active answering.
format.p2: The critical rule: **audio plays exactly once**. Unlike IELTS or TOEFL, there is no replay. Questions are strictly ordered by difficulty, so you cannot skip or go back: each answer is locked in as you move forward.
format.p3: Questions are multiple-choice but probe very different things: factual details (time, price, location), speaker attitude (for/against/neutral), implicit meaning, and logical conclusions. The further you go, the more the exam tests deep comprehension rather than surface-level keyword matching.
format.p4: Audio material includes: everyday dialogues (airport announcements, shop exchanges, phone bookings), news and interviews (RFI bulletins, guest panels, radio features), and academic or specialized lectures (economics, science, philosophy). Accents are mostly metropolitan French, with occasional Quebec or African French.

scoring.title: Scoring weight table: why questions 1-20 are not enough
scoring.intro: TCF Canada listening is not scored uniformly — hard questions are worth over 10× the easy ones. Many candidates only practice the first 20 questions and get stuck at CLB 5-6. Here is the full weight breakdown based on the official scoring model:
scoring.insight: The first 10 questions combined are worth only 9.4% of the total. Questions 20-39 — just 20 items — contribute 71.2% of your score. If you get Q1-10 perfect and only half of Q20-39 right, you end up around 412 points (CLB 7, barely). But if you ace Q20-39 and only get half of Q1-10 right, you land near 531 points (straight CLB 8). Every question after Q20 is worth several low-level questions.

a1a2.title: A1-A2 (Q1-10): Free points
a1a2.desc: Short dialogues, slow speech, clear articulation. Topics cover everyday life: asking for directions, ordering food, shopping, appointments, time, public transit announcements. Each speaker says 1-2 sentences, and the answer is usually right there in the audio.
a1a2.scenarios: Typical scenarios: airport announcements, bus stops, shop prices, phone booking, weather forecast, brief self-introduction
a1a2.sample: Sample: you hear "Le train pour Paris partira à 14h30 du quai 5" and the question asks "Quel est le numéro du quai ?" — answer is "5", just catch the number
a1a2.errors: Common mistakes: confusing the number you heard (14h30) with the number asked (quai 5); missing the preposition "pour Paris" and mixing up directions

a2b1.title: A2-B1 (Q11-19): Context starts to matter
a2b1.desc: Dialogues get longer, speech is closer to natural pace, colloquial expressions appear. Topics cover work, travel, shopping plans, invitations, complaints, short interviews. Questions shift from "what did you hear" to "what does the speaker mean" or "what will they do next".
a2b1.scenarios: Typical scenarios: co-worker chat, voicemail, customer service, simple radio shows, travel info
a2b1.sample: Sample: a conversation about why a colleague is absent. Question: "Pourquoi Marie n'est pas venue aujourd'hui ?" — the answer requires combining two sentences: "Elle m'a dit que son fils est malade" + "...donc elle reste à la maison"
a2b1.errors: Common mistakes: falling for distractors that reuse audio words (e.g. "malade" appears in the options but refers to the son, not Marie); confusing "hier" with "aujourd'hui"

b1b2.title: B1-B2 (Q20-30): The core scoring zone
b1b2.desc: Long conversations, interviews, news reports, debate excerpts. Topics become more abstract: social issues, cultural discussions, environment, technology, education policy. Speakers express opinions, compare positions, use metaphors or euphemisms. This is the highest-value continuous block (21 points per question, 231 points total) and the real dividing line between CLB 6 and CLB 8.
b1b2.scenarios: Typical scenarios: RFI news analysis, guest interviews, radio debates, expert science features, documentary narration, cultural criticism
b1b2.sample: Sample: a discussion on remote work with two guests, one for and one against. Question: "Quelle est l'opinion de l'invité masculin ?" — you need to identify tone markers ("mais", "cependant", "je ne suis pas d'accord") to distinguish positions, not just the topic
b1b2.errors: Common mistakes: missing attitude markers — "en effet", "certes...mais", "il est vrai que..." are concession patterns that flip the speaker's position. Miss them and you pick the wrong opinion

b2c1.title: B2-C2 (Q31-39): The CLB 7 vs CLB 9 decider
b2c1.desc: Academic lectures, expert roundtables, long-form reports, philosophical discussions. Fast speech, advanced vocabulary, long nested sentences. Topics from environmental economics to post-structuralism. Each question is worth 27-33 points, 9 questions totalling 267 points — this section alone can push you from CLB 7 to CLB 9, or hold you back.
b2c1.scenarios: Typical scenarios: university lecture recordings, TED-style talks, academic journal summaries, expert forums
b2c1.sample: Sample: a 90-second lecture on AI ethics. Question: "Quelle est la thèse principale de l'intervenant ?" — you need to identify the speaker's core thesis, supporting arguments, and potential counter-arguments, then pick the option that best paraphrases the main point
b2c1.errors: Common mistakes: obsessing over every word and drowning in long sentences; forgetting what was said 30 seconds ago because you took no notes; mistaking a supporting example for the main thesis

strategy.title: 35-minute stage-by-stage strategy
strategy.intro: Most candidates don't realize TCF listening has a rhythm: early questions are plentiful but low-value, later questions are scarce but high-value. Allocating attention is more important than trying to catch every word.

phase1.title: Stage 1: Q1-10 (~7 minutes)
phase1.action: Fast, confident, no hesitation. You should finish this block in 7 minutes and score at least 9/10 or perfect. Mentally shift gears immediately after.
phase1.tip: If you miss more than 3 of these, your foundation isn't solid enough — go back to A1-A2 level practice on HiTCF instead of booking the real exam.

phase2.title: Stage 2: Q11-19 (~8 minutes)
phase2.action: Crank up focus. These 9 questions are 15 points each — one mistake is manageable, but start training "listen and predict" habits.
phase2.tip: Use the 3-5 seconds before each clip to scan keywords in the 4 options and sketch 4 possible answer shapes in your head.

phase3.title: Stage 3: Q20-30 (~11 minutes)
phase3.action: Full attention. This is the 231-point core block. A single lapse costs you 21 points.
phase3.tip: Use the scratch paper (where allowed) with simple symbols: speaker stance, key numbers, times, reversal markers.

phase4.title: Stage 4: Q31-39 (~9 minutes)
phase4.action: Abandon the need to understand every word. Grab the thesis, the structure, and the counter-arguments. Skipping technical vocabulary is fine — as long as you get the logical skeleton.
phase4.tip: If a question really stumps you, pick the option that "doesn't repeat audio words" but "makes the most logical sense" — TCF correct answers are usually paraphrases, not verbatim repetitions.

traps.title: 10 high-frequency traps (with countermeasures)
traps.intro: HiTCF error data shows that 80% of listening losses concentrate on these 10 trap types. Memorizing them is more valuable than doing 10 more practice sets.

items.title: Trap 1: Number confusion
items.desc: The most common. French numbers are complex (soixante-douze=72, quatre-vingts=80, quatre-vingt-dix=90), and examiners deliberately stack several in one clip. Fix: write every number down immediately.

items.title: Trap 2: Reversed time
items.desc: Question asks "when", audio gives two times (meeting at 15h but a prep session at 14h); the distractor is 14h. Fix: grab the "final time", not the first one you heard.

items.title: Trap 3: Missed negation
items.desc: "Je ne suis pas d'accord" vs "Je suis d'accord" — at fast speed the unstressed "ne...pas" can vanish. Fix: whenever you hear an opinion verb, check for ne/pas/jamais/non.

items.title: Trap 4: Concession flip
items.desc: "Certes, X est vrai, mais Y est plus important" — the actual position is after "mais". Distractor captures the X half. Fix: treat mais/cependant/toutefois/néanmoins as stance reset buttons.

items.title: Trap 5: Homophones / near-homophones
items.desc: poisson (fish) vs poison; fois (times) vs foie (liver) vs foi (faith). Only context disambiguates. Fix: never pick an answer based purely on word sound.

items.title: Trap 6: Ambiguous pronouns
items.desc: "Elle lui a dit qu'il ne devait pas y aller" — who told whom not to go where? Two pronouns, two people. Fix: tag each subject in your head as pronouns stack up.

items.title: Trap 7: Rhetorical questions
items.desc: "Est-ce que j'ai dit que c'était facile ?" is rhetorical — it means "I did NOT say it was easy". Fix: note tone lift + surrounding frustration markers.

items.title: Trap 8: Polite refusal
items.desc: In French, "Ce n'est pas vraiment mon truc" or "Je vais y réfléchir" are polite refusals, not genuine deliberations. Fix: learn the standard French refusal phrases.

items.title: Trap 9: Details drown the thesis
items.desc: A 60-second lecture packs 3 data points, 2 examples, and 1 main argument. The question asks about the main argument, but distractors use details. Fix: write the thesis at the top of your scratch sheet, details below.

items.title: Trap 10: Speaker attitude
items.desc: Examiners love "Quelle est l'attitude de l'intervenant ?" with options like ironique / enthousiaste / sceptique / neutre. Fix: listen to pitch + adjective intensity — sarcasm speeds up, adjectives exaggerate.

keyExpressions.title: High-frequency listening expressions (4 categories)
keyExpressions.intro: These are the patterns that appear most often in TCF listening. Knowing them lets you automatically switch to "trap alert" mode during the exam.

time.name: Time
time.items: dans deux jours (in two days) · avant-hier (the day before yesterday) · la semaine prochaine (next week) · il y a trois mois (three months ago) · à partir du 5 (from the 5th) · jusqu'au 20 (until the 20th) · vers 15h (around 3pm) · pile à midi (exactly at noon)

numbers.name: Numbers
numbers.items: soixante-dix (70) · quatre-vingts (80) · quatre-vingt-dix (90) · quatre-vingt-onze (91) · la moitié (half) · le quart (a quarter) · le double (double) · un tiers (a third) · une dizaine (around ten)

negation.name: Negation / reversal
negation.items: ne...pas · ne...jamais · ne...plus · ne...aucun · au contraire · en revanche · toutefois · cependant · pourtant · mais · certes...mais · malgré · bien que + subj

tone.name: Tone / attitude
tone.items: en effet (indeed) · à vrai dire (to be honest) · franchement (frankly) · à mon avis (in my opinion) · selon moi (to me) · il me semble que (it seems to me) · je doute que (I doubt) · sans aucun doute (without a doubt) · soi-disant (so-called, ironic)

preparationPlan.title: 8-week prep plan (B1 to CLB 7+)
preparationPlan.intro: This plan assumes you already have a B1 foundation and are targeting CLB 7 (331+ points). Commit roughly one hour of listening practice per day.

week1_2.title: Weeks 1-2: Foundation sweep
week1_2.actions: 20 minutes daily of RFI Journal en français facile (slow-speed news); 4 HiTCF A1-A2 level practice sets; drill unfamiliar numbers, time expressions, and basic connectors.

week3_4.title: Weeks 3-4: Adjust to natural speed
week3_4.actions: Switch to normal-speed RFI Actualité and France Inter podcasts; 6 HiTCF A2-B1 level practice sets; start practicing "first-pass correct" discipline — no replays allowed.

week5_6.title: Weeks 5-6: Core breakthrough (B1-B2)
week5_6.actions: 10-15 HiTCF B1-B2 real questions per day; use the sentence-level replay feature to fully understand every mistake; 2 full 35-minute mock exams per week.

week7_8.title: Weeks 7-8: High-level sprint
week7_8.actions: Attack the B2-C2 block (Q31-39), 3-5 deep lecture questions daily; 3 full mock exams per week; review every B1+ mistake in your error notebook; stop practicing 3 days before the exam to preserve sharpness.

tips.title: 10 tips you can apply immediately
tips.tip1: Always read the question and all 4 options before audio starts — 5 seconds of preview beats a minute of panic during playback.
tips.tip2: Missed a sentence? Don't dwell. French answers are usually inferrable from context; fixating on one word drags down the next five questions.
tips.tip3: Lock onto tone and reversal markers: mais, cependant, en fait, à vrai dire — these are the TCF's go-to signals for speaker attitude.
tips.tip4: Beware the "exact word" trap: correct answers tend to paraphrase the audio (reformulation), while distractors recycle the original words.
tips.tip5: Listen to real French media daily: RFI, France 24, Radio-Canada, French podcasts. Real speed, no subtitles.
tips.tip6: Use symbols in your notes, not words: ↑ for agree, ↓ for disagree, ? for uncertain, △ for time, € for price.
tips.tip7: Skip unknown technical vocabulary immediately — TCF never tests jargon, only logic and the main idea.
tips.tip8: Pair listening with speaking training: shadow 3 minutes of RFI news before a listening set, and your ear adapts faster.
tips.tip9: Don't do new questions the night before the exam. Review only your error notebook — new wrong answers crush confidence.
tips.tip10: If the venue allows scratch paper, use it. If not, trace digits and times on the desk with your finger — muscle memory helps.

practice.title: How to practice listening efficiently on HiTCF
practice.p1: HiTCF has 42 complete TCF Canada listening test sets (1,638 questions). Every audio clip was processed with OpenAI Whisper to generate sentence-level timestamps — meaning when you review a mistake, you can replay exactly the sentence you missed instead of re-listening to the entire 90-second clip. No other TCF platform offers this.
practice.p2: Recommended 3-phase practice flow: (1) **Practice Mode** — replay allowed, transcripts visible, goal is to understand every sentence; ideal for beginners. (2) **Level Practice** — pick a specific A1-C2 band to target your weak spot; ideal mid-prep. (3) **Exam Mode** — 35 minutes, one pass, no replay; ideal for the final 2 weeks.
practice.p3: After every session, check the "accuracy by level" chart on the dashboard. If any level is below 60%, drop everything else and drill that band. The error notebook auto-groups your mistakes by level and error type — a single pass through it is often 3× more effective than blindly grinding new sets.

q1.q: Does TCF Canada listening audio really play only once?
q1.a: Yes. This is the biggest difference from DELF/DALF and most other French exams. The testing system offers no replay button, and once an answer is locked in you cannot revisit it. Train with a "one-pass" mindset from day one.

q2.q: How many listening points do I need for CLB 7?
q2.a: CLB 7 requires at least 331 listening points (out of 699). CLB 8 needs 369 and CLB 9 needs 449. Most Canadian PR pathways require CLB 7.

q3.q: Can I take notes during the listening section?
q3.a: Policies vary by test center. Most official centers allow short notes on scratch paper (provided by the center), but you cannot pre-write anything. Confirm with your specific venue before test day.

q4.q: How long do I need to practice daily to hit CLB 7?
q4.a: If you already have a B1 base, 45-60 minutes of targeted listening (real questions + French media) over 8 weeks is typically enough. From scratch, expect at least 6 months of 2 hours per day of systematic study.

q5.q: Do Quebec and metropolitan French accents both appear?
q5.a: The exam is mostly metropolitan French (~80%), with occasional Canadian (Quebec) and African French accents in interviews or news clips. Spend some time with Radio-Canada to acclimate.

q6.q: What should I do when I miss a sentence?
q6.a: Drop it instantly and redirect attention to the rest of the clip. The biggest TCF listening mistake is "getting stuck on one sentence" — a minute later you've missed the entire question. Most answers are inferrable from context.

q7.q: What is HiTCF's sentence-level timestamp feature?
q7.a: We process every audio clip with OpenAI Whisper to generate sentence-level timestamps. During practice or error review, you click any sentence to jump the audio to exactly that spot, instead of replaying the entire 90-second segment. No other TCF prep platform offers this.

q8.q: How should I prepare the week before the exam?
q8.a: Days 7 to 3: one full 35-minute mock per day plus error review. Days 2 and 1: only review the error notebook, no new questions. Night before: sleep early; 30 minutes of RFI news is enough to keep your ear warm. Don't introduce new material.

tcfReading.heading: TCF Canada Reading (CE) Complete Guide: How to Hit 699 Points in 60 Minutes
tcfReading.intro: TCF Canada Reading (Compréhension écrite, or CE) looks gentler than Listening — 60 minutes for 39 questions, and you can revise answers — but losses concentrate at the back end: the first 10 questions are easy but low-value, while the last 20 carry long texts, dense vocabulary, and layered traps. This guide uses the real scoring data from HiTCF's 42 test sets (1,638 real questions) to give you a scoring weight table, a 60-minute allocation plan, 10 common trap types, a high-frequency connector glossary, and an 8-week study plan.

quickFacts.title: Exam at a glance (memorize these 5 numbers)

format.title: Exam format: 60 minutes, back-and-forth allowed, but texts get denser
format.p1: The TCF Canada reading section has 39 multiple-choice questions, each with 4 options (A/B/C/D). Total time is 60 minutes — about 3 minutes of instructions and 57 minutes of active answering.
format.p2: The biggest difference from Listening: **you can revisit and change answers**. This sounds like a gift, but it also means you control the clock — and without audio to push you along, it's easy to spend 5 minutes on one hard question and discover 20 more waiting.
format.p3: Question format is uniform (multiple choice) but text types vary wildly: notices, ads, menus (A1), emails, blogs, product descriptions (A2-B1), newspaper editorials, feature reports (B1-B2), academic papers, literary excerpts, legal documents (B2-C2). As you progress, texts get longer and syntax becomes more nested.
format.p4: Question targets: factual information (time/place/price), main idea, author stance, implicit meaning, logical relationships (cause/concession/contrast), synonymic rewording. High-level questions (C1-C2) barely test literal comprehension — they're almost entirely about inference and rhetorical recognition.

scoring.title: Scoring weight table: why the B2 band decides everything
scoring.intro: Based on real scoring data from HiTCF's 1,638 reading questions (pulled directly from all 42 test sets), TCF Canada reading distributes points as follows. Note that C1 questions are worth 26 points each (listening's C1 is 27) — this is an official weighting specific to reading:
scoring.insight: The B2 block (Q20-29, 10 questions for 210 points) is the real scoring zone — 30% of the total. C1+C2 combined add another 41.2% (288 points), which is actually more weight than the equivalent high-level listening band. Reading demands more high-level ability than listening: to hit CLB 8+, you must handle C1-C2 long texts reliably; you cannot coast on the first 19 questions, which together contribute only 28.7% of the score.

a1a2.title: A1-A2 (Q1-10): Free 66 points
a1a2.desc: Short, practical texts: signs, menus, brief ads, text messages, schedules, metro maps. Sentences are usually under 15 words, vocabulary is everyday. Questions are direct: "What's the price?" "When does it open?" "What kind of ad is this?"
a1a2.scenarios: Typical scenarios: supermarket promo posters, restaurant menus, metro/bus timetables, tourist site summaries, cinema tickets, email notifications
a1a2.sample: Sample: a cinema poster says "Séances du vendredi au dimanche : 14h, 17h, 20h30. Tarif réduit le mercredi." Question: "Quand peut-on voir un film à prix réduit ?" — answer: mercredi. Pure keyword location
a1a2.errors: Common mistakes: missing "du ... au ..." as a time range; confusing prix réduit (discount) with plein tarif (full price); reading the wrong weekday (vendredi vs jeudi)

a2b1.title: A2-B1 (Q11-19): Medium texts, main idea starts to matter
a2b1.desc: Text length doubles (100-200 words): personal blogs, travel stories, product descriptions, simple news, forum Q&A. Compound sentences appear, simple subordinate clauses, some colloquial expressions. Questions shift from "find the fact" to "what's this paragraph really about" and "how does the author feel".
a2b1.scenarios: Typical scenarios: lifestyle blogs, product reviews, travel journals, forum help posts, simple news, work emails
a2b1.sample: Sample: a blog about the author's first year in Lyon — some positives, some negatives. Question: "Quel est le sentiment général de l'auteur ?" — you can't just grab one paragraph; you need to synthesize "weather is bad" + "people are friendly" + "food is meh" + "lifestyle is relaxed" and pick "overall satisfied, with reservations" rather than "likes" or "dislikes"
a2b1.errors: Common mistakes: jumping to a conclusion from the first or last paragraph (TCF loves mid-text reversals); confusing an example for the main point; ignoring the true stance that appears after mais/pourtant/cependant

b1b2.title: B1-B2 (Q20-29): Core scoring zone (210 points)
b1b2.desc: Text length 250-400 words: newspaper editorials, feature reports, formal letters, policy briefs, interviews, cultural commentary. Vocabulary becomes abstract (policy, opinion, comparison), sentences get long with nested clauses. Questions focus heavily on "what the author actually thinks" and "what does this imply" and "how do two speakers differ".
b1b2.scenarios: Typical scenarios: Le Monde editorials, La Croix columns, Le Figaro interviews, government policy notes, cultural critiques, long-form popular science
b1b2.sample: Sample: an editorial on remote work. First half lists benefits, second half says "mais il ne faut pas sous-estimer les risques d'isolement et de surcharge mentale." Question: "Quelle est la position de l'auteur ?" — the answer is not "supports" or "opposes" but "acknowledges benefits while warning of costs". Grab everything after "mais"
b1b2.errors: Common mistakes: being misled by the positive first half; failing to recognize "il ne faut pas sous-estimer" (don't underestimate = take seriously); mistaking "both sides mentioned" for "neutral" (the author's real stance is on the second half)

b2c1.title: B2-C2 (Q30-39): The CLB 7 vs CLB 9 decider
b2c1.desc: Text length 400-600 words: academic paper excerpts, literary works (Camus, Maupassant, Le Clézio), legal provisions, philosophical essays, economic analyses, scientific reviews. Sentences can be 40+ words with nested clauses, complex rhetoric (irony, metaphor, wordplay). Vocabulary is academic and specialized, hard to infer from context. Each question is worth 26-33 points; 10 questions total 288 points — this block caps your ceiling.
b2c1.scenarios: Typical scenarios: university textbook excerpts, classic literature snippets, legal rulings, philosophical essays, scientific journal abstracts, economic reports
b2c1.sample: Sample: the opening of Camus's L'Étranger. Question: "Quel effet l'auteur cherche-t-il à produire avec la phrase 'Aujourd'hui, maman est morte' ?" — not about the literal meaning (too simple), but about rhetorical intent: options include "emphasize grief", "show detachment", "stress the mundane", "elicit pity". You need to recognize Camus's existentialist style
b2c1.errors: Common mistakes: trying to translate every word and losing the rhythm; failing to spot irony ("quelle belle journée" in a dark context = sarcastic); confusing the author's position with the opposing view the author cites; reading "nul ne peut" (no one may — absolute prohibition) as "no one can"

strategy.title: 60-minute time allocation strategy
strategy.intro: The biggest difference from listening is that you control the clock. But that's a double-edged sword — candidates without pacing discipline hemorrhage time on hard questions. Here's the optimal allocation based on real scoring weights:

phase1.title: Stage 1: Q1-10 (target 8 minutes)
phase1.action: 48 seconds per question. This block should be nearly instant — read the question → scan for the keyword → check options → pick. Do not closely read the entire text; only look around the keyword. Finishing 66 points in 8 minutes is your first checkpoint.
phase1.tip: If this block takes over 12 minutes, your basic vocabulary isn't solid enough. Go back to A1-A2 word lists before booking the real exam.

phase2.title: Stage 2: Q11-19 (target 14 minutes)
phase2.action: About 1.5 minutes per question. Texts get longer, so scan paragraph structure (first sentence + last sentence + topic sentences) before diving for the answer. You should be at 22 minutes total, 201/699 points.
phase2.tip: Train the habit of "read halfway, jump to the question to see what's asked" — many TCF questions only need one paragraph of the text, not the whole thing.

phase3.title: Stage 3: Q20-29 (target 18 minutes)
phase3.action: About 1.8 minutes per question. This is the 210-point core block — don't skip. Read the argument structure fully: introduction → evidence → pivot → conclusion. If scratch paper is allowed, map each paragraph's core sentence. Cumulative time: 40 minutes, 411/699 points.
phase3.tip: If one question truly stumps you, mark it (write ? next to the number) and move on, but come back. A 21-point blank is the most expensive "no answer" in the reading section.

phase4.title: Stage 4: Q30-39 (target 18 minutes + 2 minutes review)
phase4.action: About 1.8 minutes per question. High-level block — abandon the need to understand every word and grab the argument skeleton and tone instead. Skip unknown technical vocabulary; judge positive/negative from context. Save 2 minutes at the end to revisit marked (?) questions, prioritizing B2 band (21-point questions have higher revision ROI than C1's 26-point questions because B2 errors are often careless).
phase4.tip: The C2 block (Q36-39, 4 questions = 132 points) is the hardest. If time runs short, "strategic guessing" beats leaving blanks — 25% vs 0%. Don't spend 10 minutes grinding the last question.

traps.title: 10 high-frequency reading traps (with countermeasures)
traps.intro: HiTCF error data shows 70% of reading losses concentrate on these 10 traps. Unlike listening traps, reading traps live more in "vocabulary" and "logic" layers.

items.title: Trap 1: False friends (faux amis)
items.desc: actuel ≠ actual (means "current", not "real"); sensible ≠ sensitive (means "sensible"); assister ≠ assist (means "attend"); librairie ≠ library (means "bookshop"; library = bibliothèque). Fix: memorize a standard false-friends list and stay alert every time you see them.

items.title: Trap 2: Ambiguous references
items.desc: "Cela", "celui-ci", "lequel", "dont", "y", "en" all replace a prior noun or concept. Misreading the antecedent breaks the question. Fix: whenever a pronoun appears, scroll back and confirm what it refers to.

items.title: Trap 3: Negation scope
items.desc: "Je ne crois pas qu'il soit heureux" (I don't believe he's happy) ≠ "I believe he's not happy" (semantically distinct). "ne...que" means "only" — it's affirmative, not negative. Fix: when you see negation, ask what exactly is being negated.

items.title: Trap 4: Concession flip
items.desc: "Bien que / Quoique / Même si + subj" structures mean "although... [but]...". The real position is in the main clause. "Il est certes intelligent, mais paresseux" — the author's verdict is "lazy", not "smart". Fix: treat bien que / certes / malgré as "ignore this first part" signals.

items.title: Trap 5: Conditional tense traps
items.desc: "Si j'avais su, je serais venu" (past unreal: I didn't come) ≠ "Si je sais, je viens" (real hypothesis). Tense determines whether something actually happened. Fix: when you see "si", first identify the verb tense.

items.title: Trap 6: Inverted subject/object
items.desc: French relative clauses can invert: "Le livre que Paul a donné à Marie" — who gave what to whom? Grab the main subject (Paul), verb (donné), indirect object (à Marie). Fix: trace subject-verb-object with your finger on the page for complex clauses.

items.title: Trap 7: Time adverbs implying change
items.desc: "autrefois" (formerly) vs "de nos jours" (nowadays) — time contrasts usually mean the author is discussing change, and questions may ask "does the author miss the past?" Fix: as soon as you spot time contrast words, identify the author's stance.

items.title: Trap 8: Rhetorical questions
items.desc: "Comment peut-on accepter cela ?" is rhetorical — it means "this is unacceptable". "Qui n'a jamais rêvé de ...?" = "everyone has dreamed of...". Fix: every question mark needs a quick check — is this a real question or a statement in disguise?

items.title: Trap 9: Options all appear in the text
items.desc: Classic TCF trap: options A and B both quote words from the passage, but only one accurately reflects the author's overall stance. Fix: when comparing options, ask what context each phrase appeared in — mere appearance doesn't make it the author's view.

items.title: Trap 10: Over-inference
items.desc: An option is plausible and "sounds right" but isn't actually supported by the text. TCF only tests "conclusions the text supports", not "world knowledge". Fix: before picking an answer, return to the text and find explicit support; if you can't, don't pick it.

keyExpressions.title: High-frequency connectors and structures (4 categories)
keyExpressions.intro: The hidden scoring point in reading is "connector recognition" — these words are the logical skeleton of any text. Below are the four most frequent structure families in TCF reading:

time.name: Time / sequence
time.items: auparavant (previously) · désormais (from now on) · jadis (long ago) · actuellement (currently) · à l'avenir (in the future) · entre-temps (meanwhile) · dès lors (from then on) · peu à peu (gradually) · tout à coup (suddenly) · par la suite (subsequently)

numbers.name: Cause / conclusion
numbers.items: donc · par conséquent · ainsi · c'est pourquoi · de ce fait · en conséquence · par suite de · en raison de · grâce à · à cause de · faute de

negation.name: Concession / reversal
negation.items: cependant · toutefois · néanmoins · pourtant · en revanche · par contre · alors que · tandis que · bien que + subj · quoique + subj · malgré · en dépit de · certes...mais

tone.name: Attitude / stance
tone.items: à mon avis · selon moi · d'après · il me semble que · il est évident que · sans aucun doute · paraît-il · soi-disant · prétendument · il est à noter que · on peut déplorer que · il convient de souligner

preparationPlan.title: 8-week preparation plan (B1 to CLB 7+)
preparationPlan.intro: This plan assumes a B1 foundation and targets CLB 7 (331+ reading points). Commit roughly one hour of reading practice per day.

week1_2.title: Weeks 1-2: Vocabulary sweep + basic texts
week1_2.actions: Memorize 30 A2-B1 high-frequency words daily (focus: false friends, connectors, tense markers); 4 HiTCF A1-A2 level reading sets; read one RFI en français facile or 1 Jour 1 Actu (kids' news) article per day.

week3_4.title: Weeks 3-4: Medium-text adaptation
week3_4.actions: Switch to regular Le Monde, Libération, France 24 articles; 6 HiTCF A2-B1 reading sets; practice the "question-first, text-second" exam strategy.

week5_6.title: Weeks 5-6: Core breakthrough (B1-B2)
week5_6.actions: 10-15 HiTCF B1-B2 reading questions daily; focus on editorial (opinion/éditorial) reading to train stance identification; 2 full 60-minute mock exams per week.

week7_8.title: Weeks 7-8: High-level sprint (B2-C2)
week7_8.actions: Attack the C1-C2 band: one Le Monde front-page long read or one literary passage per day (Camus, Saint-Exupéry, Le Clézio); 3 full mock exams per week; error-notebook review focused on B2 band (21 pts/question).

tips.title: 10 tips you can apply immediately
tips.tip1: Read the question before the text. 70% of TCF reading questions only need one paragraph; knowing what you're hunting for is 3× more efficient than reading front-to-back.
tips.tip2: When skimming, fix on the first and last sentences of each paragraph — they're usually topic sentences telling you what that paragraph is about.
tips.tip3: Don't stop on unknown words. Use word families (emploi/employé/employer) and affixes (in-, dé-, re-, -tion, -ment) to infer meaning. If you really can't, skip — it rarely affects the answer.
tips.tip4: Lock onto reversal words: mais, cependant, toutefois, en revanche, néanmoins, pourtant — these are stance-flip signals that TCF loves to test.
tips.tip5: Beware false friends: actuel, sensible, assister, éventuellement, librairie, etc. They look English but mean something different — high-frequency exam traps.
tips.tip6: Enforce time allocation: A1-A2 = 1 min, B1 = 1.5 min, B2-C2 = 1.8-2 min per question. If you exceed, mark and move on.
tips.tip7: Every answer must have direct textual support — never rely on "common sense" or "what I think is likely". TCF tests the text, not the world.
tips.tip8: Use symbols for notes: ↗ agree, ↘ disagree, ? pivot, ! important, → cause-effect. On scratch paper (if allowed), sketch paragraph structure.
tips.tip9: In the high-level block (C1-C2), give up on understanding every word. Grab the argument skeleton and tone. Skipping technical vocabulary rarely breaks a question.
tips.tip10: Never leave a question blank. If time runs out, guess — TCF doesn't penalize wrong answers. 25% chance beats 0%.

practice.title: How to practice reading efficiently on HiTCF
practice.p1: HiTCF has 42 complete TCF Canada reading test sets with 1,638 real questions, each accompanied by a **per-option explanation** — not just "the answer is B" but why A is wrong (paragraph X says the opposite), why B is right (paragraph Y explicitly states Z), and why C is a trap (it uses a word from the text but in a different meaning). This is rare on other TCF platforms, and for a comprehension-heavy exam like reading, explanation quality matters far more than raw question count.
practice.p2: Recommended 3-phase flow: (1) **Practice Mode** — slow reading, explanations visible, vocabulary lookups allowed, goal is to understand every text's logical skeleton; ideal for beginners. (2) **Level Practice** — target a weak band (e.g., if your B1 accuracy is under 60%, drill only B1); ideal mid-prep. (3) **Exam Mode** — 60-minute timed run, simulating real pressure; ideal for the final 2 weeks.
practice.p3: After every session, check the "accuracy by level" chart. Reading weak points often differ from listening weak points — many candidates are strong on listening B2 but weak on reading B2 (because reading demands more vocabulary and long-sentence parsing). Group your errors by type in the notebook (false friend / negation scope / ambiguous reference) and drill by type — this is 3-5× more efficient than grinding random new sets.

q1.q: Can I revise my answers on TCF Canada Reading?
q1.a: Yes. This is the biggest difference from listening. Within the 60 minutes you can skip questions, come back, and change answers. This gives you flexibility — but also demands discipline, because you cannot afford to grind a single hard question.

q2.q: How many reading points do I need for CLB 7?
q2.a: CLB 7 requires at least 331 reading points out of 699. CLB 8 needs 379 and CLB 9 needs 453. Most Canadian PR pathways require CLB 7.

q3.q: Can I use a dictionary during the exam?
q3.a: No. TCF Canada does not allow any reference materials — no dictionaries, phones, or notes. Only the scratch paper and pen provided by the test center.

q4.q: I read slowly in French. What can I do?
q4.a: Reading speed is trainable. Every day, time yourself reading one French news article (start at 10 minutes, gradually compress to 5); practice skimming (first/last sentences only); do HiTCF's timed level-practice drills. 8 weeks typically improves reading speed by 40-60%.

q5.q: Are all texts in standard French?
q5.a: The vast majority are in standard French (français standard). Occasionally you'll see Canadian or African French expressions — usually in dialogue or literary excerpts. Formal texts are nearly all standard French.

q6.q: What if I've never read the literary works used in C1-C2?
q6.a: You don't need to. TCF doesn't test literary history — only whether you can understand the passage. Even when a text comes from Camus or Saint-Exupéry, the question is about in-passage content, tone, or rhetoric, not background context.

q7.a: Every question includes a **per-option breakdown**: why A is wrong (because paragraph X contradicts it), why B is right (paragraph Y explicitly states it), why C is a trap (uses the original wording in a different meaning). For a comprehension exam, this is far more valuable than a raw answer key.

q8.q: How should I prepare the week before the exam?
q8.a: Days 7 to 3: one full 60-minute mock per day plus error review. Days 2 and 1: review only the error notebook's high-frequency trap types (false friends, negation, references, concession) — no new questions. Night before: sleep early; 20 minutes of Le Monde is enough to keep your French warm.

tcfSpeaking.heading: TCF Canada Speaking (EO) — Complete Practice Guide
tcfSpeaking.intro: The Speaking section (Expression orale, or EO) is a face-to-face interview lasting approximately 12 minutes, recorded and evaluated by certified examiners. It tests your ability to communicate in French across three progressively challenging tasks. Many candidates find this the most stressful section, but structured preparation can make a huge difference.

format.title: Exam Structure: 3 Tasks
format.p1: The speaking exam consists of three tasks of increasing difficulty. You interact with an examiner who guides the conversation. The entire session is audio-recorded for evaluation by multiple raters.

task1.title: Task 1: Guided Interview (2 minutes)
task1.desc: The examiner asks you questions about yourself — your background, daily life, interests, work, and studies. This is the most predictable section and targets A1-B1 level. You should answer in complete sentences, provide details and examples, and show you can sustain a basic conversation. Prepare answers for common topics: family, hometown, hobbies, job/studies, daily routine, and future plans.

task2.title: Task 2: Role Play (3.5 minutes)
task2.desc: You receive a scenario card describing a situation where you must interact with the examiner (who plays a specific role). Common scenarios include: making a complaint at a store, negotiating with a landlord, resolving a workplace conflict, or organizing an event. This targets B1-B2 level. You need to be persuasive, handle unexpected responses, and adapt your language to the situation.

task3.title: Task 3: Expressing a Point of View (4.5 minutes)
task3.desc: You are given a topic or document and must express and defend your opinion. The examiner will challenge your views with counter-arguments. This targets B2-C2 level. You need to structure your argument logically, provide concrete examples, acknowledge opposing viewpoints, and use a wide range of vocabulary and grammatical structures. Topics typically involve social, cultural, or environmental issues.

scoring.title: How Speaking Is Scored
scoring.p1: Examiners evaluate your performance on a grid covering: lexical range and accuracy, grammatical range and accuracy, fluency and coherence, pronunciation and intonation, and sociolinguistic appropriateness. Each criterion contributes to your overall NCLC level.
scoring.p2: To reach NCLC 7 in speaking, you need to demonstrate: ability to discuss abstract topics with reasonable fluency, use a range of vocabulary accurately, produce complex sentences with generally correct grammar, have clear pronunciation with appropriate intonation, and adapt your register to different situations.

tips.title: Speaking Tips & Strategies
tips.tip1: Speak clearly and at a natural pace. It is better to speak slowly and correctly than quickly with many errors. Examiners are trained to evaluate quality, not speed.
tips.tip2: Use discourse markers and connectors to structure your speech: d'abord, ensuite, en revanche, par conséquent, cependant. This makes your speech sound more organized and raises your coherence score.
tips.tip3: Don't be afraid to self-correct. If you realize you made a grammatical mistake, briefly correct yourself and move on. This shows awareness of accuracy and is viewed positively by examiners.
tips.tip4: For Task 3, always structure your response: introduce your position, give 2-3 arguments with examples, address the counterargument, and conclude. Even if your French isn't perfect, a well-structured response scores higher.
tips.tip5: Practice speaking aloud daily. Record yourself, listen back, and identify areas for improvement. Use HiTCF's AI conversation feature to simulate the examiner interaction and get instant feedback on pronunciation and fluency.

preparation.title: Effective Preparation Methods
preparation.p1: Use HiTCF's pronunciation practice to record yourself reading French texts and get real-time AI assessment of your accuracy and fluency at the phoneme level. This builds the muscle memory and confidence you need for the actual exam.
preparation.p2: For conversational practice, HiTCF's AI Conversation feature simulates Tâche 2 and Tâche 3 with an AI examiner. You practice speaking on real exam topics, receive 6-dimension scoring, and can repeat scenarios to improve. This is the closest experience to the real exam you can get from home.

tcfWriting.heading: TCF Canada Writing (EE) — Complete Practice Guide
tcfWriting.intro: The Writing section (Expression écrite, or EE) requires you to produce three written texts of increasing complexity in 60 minutes. Unlike the multiple-choice sections, writing is evaluated holistically by trained examiners, so understanding the scoring criteria and mastering the right methodology is key to a high score.

format.title: Exam Structure: 3 Tasks
format.p1: The writing exam consists of three tasks that test progressively more advanced writing skills. You write on paper (or on a computer at some test centers), and your responses are evaluated by multiple certified examiners.

task1.title: Task 1: Short Message (60-120 words, ~15 minutes)
task1.desc: Write a message, email, or short letter in response to a given situation. Common scenarios include: responding to an invitation, writing to a landlord about a maintenance issue, contacting a colleague about a schedule change, or replying to a job listing. This targets A1-B1 level. Focus on: using the appropriate register (tu/vous), including all required information, and following standard French letter/email conventions.

task2.title: Task 2: Article or Formal Letter (120-150 words, ~20 minutes)
task2.desc: Write an article, blog post, or formal letter on a given topic. You may be asked to describe an experience, explain a phenomenon, or argue for a position. This targets B1-B2 level. You need a clear introduction, well-developed body paragraphs, and a conclusion. Demonstrate varied vocabulary, accurate grammar (past tenses, subjunctive, conditional), and logical organization with appropriate connectors.

task3.title: Task 3: Comparative Essay (120-180 words, ~25 minutes)
task3.desc: Analyze and compare two perspectives on a given topic, then express your own opinion. You typically receive two short texts presenting opposing viewpoints (e.g., remote work vs. office work, technology in education). This targets B2-C2 level. You must: summarize each viewpoint fairly, identify key similarities and differences, present your own reasoned opinion with evidence, and write a balanced conclusion. This is where strong argumentation and sophisticated language use matter most.

scoring.title: How Writing Is Scored
scoring.p1: Examiners evaluate each task on criteria including: sociolinguistic competence (appropriate register, conventions), lexical competence (vocabulary range and precision), grammatical competence (accuracy and complexity of structures), and pragmatic/discourse competence (organization, coherence, argumentation).
scoring.p2: To reach NCLC 7 in writing, you need to demonstrate: clear text organization with logical paragraph structure, varied vocabulary used accurately, generally correct grammar with complex structures (subjunctive, conditional, relative clauses), appropriate register for each task type, and the ability to develop and support an argument.

tips.title: Writing Tips & Strategies
tips.tip1: Plan before you write. Spend 2-3 minutes outlining your main points for each task. A well-organized response with simple French scores higher than a disorganized response with complex French.
tips.tip2: Respect the word count. Writing significantly under or over the limit can affect your score. For Task 1, aim for 80-100 words; for Task 2, 130-140 words; for Task 3, 150-170 words.
tips.tip3: Use varied connectors: cependant, néanmoins, en outre, par ailleurs, en effet, de surcroît, toutefois. This shows lexical range and improves the coherence of your text.
tips.tip4: Show off your grammar range deliberately. Use at least one conditional (Si j'avais...), one subjunctive (Il est important que...), and relative clauses (ce qui/ce que) in your longer tasks. Examiners look for evidence of complex structures.
tips.tip5: Always proofread. Reserve 5 minutes at the end to check for: gender/number agreements, verb conjugation errors, missing accents, and unclear sentences. Catching 3-4 errors in proofreading can raise your score by a full NCLC level.

methodology.title: Methodology for Each Task Type
methodology.p1: For Task 1 (message), use a clear template: greeting appropriate to the context, state the purpose of your message, provide necessary details or requests, and close with an appropriate formula (Cordialement, Bien à vous, Amicalement). For Task 2 (article/letter), follow the introduction-body-conclusion structure: hook the reader, develop 2-3 points with examples, and wrap up with a memorable closing statement.
methodology.p2: For Task 3 (comparative essay), use this proven structure: Introduction (present the topic and announce your plan) -> First viewpoint (summarize and comment) -> Second viewpoint (summarize and comment) -> Your opinion (with reasons and examples) -> Conclusion (synthesize and open). Master this framework and you can apply it to any topic.
